elements.sent-2411-001: Specialized datasets that capture the fashion industry's rich language and styling elements can boost progress in AI-driven fashion design.
elements.sent-2411-002: We present FLORA (Fashion Language Outfit Representation for Apparel Generation), the first comprehensive dataset containing 4,330 curated pairs of fashion outfits and corresponding textual descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-003: Each description utilizes industry-specific terminology and jargon commonly used by professional fashion designers, providing precise and detailed insights into the outfits.
elements.sent-2411-004: Hence, the dataset captures the delicate features and subtle stylistic elements necessary to create high-fidelity fashion designs.
elements.sent-2411-005: We demonstrate that fine-tuning generative models on the FLORA dataset significantly enhances their capability to generate accurate and stylistically rich images from textual descriptions of fashion sketches.
elements.sent-2411-006: FLORA will catalyze the creation of advanced AI models capable of comprehending and producing subtle, stylistically rich fashion designs.
elements.sent-2411-007: It will also help fashion designers and end-users to bring their ideas to life.
elements.sent-2411-008: As a second orthogonal contribution, we introduce KAN Adapters, which leverage Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KAN) as adaptive modules.
elements.sent-2411-009: They serve as replacements for traditional MLP-based LoRA adapters.
elements.sent-2411-010: With learnable spline-based activations, KAN Adapters excel in modeling complex, non-linear relationships, achieving superior fidelity, faster convergence and semantic alignment.
elements.sent-2411-011: Extensive experiments and ablation studies on our proposed FLORA dataset validate the superiority of KAN Adapters over LoRA adapters.
elements.sent-2411-012: To foster further research and collaboration, we will open-source both the FLORA and our implementation code.
elements.sent-2411-013: Artificial intelligence has become increasingly influential in various industries, fashion being a prominent example.
elements.sent-2411-014: The global fashion industry, valued at approximately $2.4$ trillion and growing at an annual rate of $5.5\%$ over the past decade \cite{mckinsey2022stateoffashion , represents a significant economic sector facing constant pressure to innovate and respond to consumer demands.}
elements.sent-2411-015: The integration of AI in fashion has revolutionized design processes and enhanced creativity and consumer personalization.
elements.sent-2411-016: AI's role in fashion extends beyond virtual try-on systems to various applications, including trend forecasting, product development, supply chain optimization, and sustainable practices \cite{sustfashion .}
elements.sent-2411-017: For instance, AI algorithms analyze vast datasets from social media and fashion shows to predict upcoming trends, enabling brands to remain competitive.
elements.sent-2411-018: Additionally, AI-powered recommendation systems provide personalized styling suggestions, enhancing the overall shopping experience for consumers.
elements.sent-2411-019: Despite the potential, AI applications in fashion face several challenges \cite{8601258 .}
elements.sent-2411-020: One major issue is the complexity of fashion design, which involves subjective details like color, style, fabric, and texture.
elements.sent-2411-021: AI models often struggle to capture the nuanced details of fashion sketches.
elements.sent-2411-022: Existing models often produce outputs that lack coherence or fail to align with the provided descriptions \cite{surveytext2img,KhushbooPatel2024,foong2023challengesimagegenerationmodels .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Another challenge is the unavailability of well-annotated datasets, especially sketch-based datasets.
elements.sent-2411-024: Traditional fashion design practices, including seasonal collections and lengthy production cycles, often lag behind the fast-paced demands of consumers.
elements.sent-2411-025: As trends evolve rapidly, designers face the challenge of producing timely collections that resonate with audiences.
elements.sent-2411-026: This underscores the need for innovations that streamline design processes to maintain competitiveness.
elements.sent-2411-027: This research addresses the problem of generating high-fidelity fashion outfit sketches based on detailed text descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-028: Unlike virtual try-on systems \cite{10213129 , which simulate how garments fit on a user, our goal is to create accurate and visually appealing fashion sketches from textual information.}
elements.sent-2411-029: Our paper addresses the critical issue of dataset quality by providing a meticulously curated and annotated dataset.
elements.sent-2411-030: Developing a robust pipeline for sketch generation from text can help overcome obstacles in translating descriptive text into coherent and stylistically appropriate visual representations.
elements.sent-2411-031: With this, we aim to advance the capability of AI in fashion design to streamline the design workflow, reducing the time spent on initial sketches, enhancing collaboration, and boosting sales.
elements.sent-2411-032: This will help emerging designers who may not have traditional sketching skills and can serve as a valuable tool for designers, researchers, and other stakeholders in this industry.
elements.sent-2411-033: Additionally, in today’s landscape, datasets are increasingly complex, with higher levels of non-linearity that make it challenging for models to effectively capture intricate patterns.
elements.sent-2411-034: Models often struggle to adapt to these complexities.
elements.sent-2411-035: For instance, datasets like FLORA present unique challenges due to their emphasis on fine-grained details—such as fabric type, fit, and decorative elements—which require nuanced understanding and multi-modal alignment.
elements.sent-2411-036: These subtle attributes introduce high variability and complexity, making it difficult for models to generalize well from limited data.
elements.sent-2411-037: To address this, we propose the KAN Adapter, which excels at learning non-linearities through its learnable activations, thereby enhancing the model’s ability to capture complex, fine-grained relationships.
elements.sent-2411-038: This paper makes the following key contributions:.
elements.sent-2411-039: FLORA Dataset: We introduce FLORA, a large-scale, curated dataset comprising 4,330 pairs of fashion outfit sketches and detailed textual descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-040: Each description uses industry-specific terminology, capturing the nuanced design elements essential for high-fidelity fashion sketch generation from text.
elements.sent-2411-041: FLORA fills a critical gap in existing resources by providing the first dataset tailored specifically to text-to-fashion sketch generation.
elements.sent-2411-042: FLORA will enable AI models to generate precise, high-quality fashion sketches from textual descriptions, reducing reliance on traditional sketching skills and accelerating the initial design phases.
elements.sent-2411-043: KAN Adapters: We propose KAN adapters, a new approach that replaces traditional MLP-based LoRA adapters with Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) \cite{liu2024kankolmogorovarnoldnetworks .}
elements.sent-2411-044: By enabling complex, non-linear transformation modeling, KAN Adapters achieve training efficiency and adaptability, making them particularly effective for tasks requiring intricate representation.
elements.sent-2411-045: Our experiments show that KAN adapters achieve faster convergence and higher fidelity than LoRA, enabling models to reach optimal performance in fewer training steps.
elements.sent-2411-046: Additionally, fine-tuning generative models on the FLORA dataset greatly improves their ability to produce accurate, stylistically rich images from fashion outfit descriptions.

elements.sent-2411-098: As a second orthogonal contribution, we propose KAN adapters.
elements.sent-2411-099: In this Section, we first provide a background on LoRA and KAN modules (Sections \ref{sec:lora_preliminary -\ref{sec:KANbackground ), and then discuss our proposed KAN adapter (Section \ref{sec:KANadapter ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-100: LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) modules provide an efficient way to fine-tune large models by optimizing the number of trainable parameters.
elements.sent-2411-101: Instead of adjusting all parameters, which can lead to ``catastrophic forgetting'', LoRA adds trainable adapters that preserve the model's learned features while achieving similar or improved results.
elements.sent-2411-102: These adapters work by training a new set of weights in a reduced dimension (or rank) that are scaled down based on model size.
elements.sent-2411-103: During fine-tuning, the model's original layers are frozen.
elements.sent-2411-104: LoRA's linear adapters process the input in parallel, scaling it down to a lower rank and then back to the output dimension.
elements.sent-2411-105: This setup prevents increased inference latency and makes it suitable for various downstream tasks.
elements.sent-2411-106: A formal representation of the LoRA is given in the equation \begin{equation}L'(x) = L(x) + W_{\text{up}} \left( \text{GeLU}\left( W_{\text{down}}(x) \right)\right)\end{equation} where \( W_{\text{up \in \mathbb{R ^{(d,l) \) and \( W_{\text{down \in \mathbb{R ^{(l,d) \), with \( l < d \).}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-107: Here, \( W_{\text{up \) and \( W_{\text{down \) represent the LoRA weights, \( L' (x) \) represents the modified layer output, and \( L(x) \) represents the original layer output to which LoRA is applied.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-108: The matrix \( W_{\text{down \) is initialized with the distribution of the weights of \( L(x) \) to maintain coherence with the frozen layer.}}
elements.sent-2411-109: Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) \cite{liu2024kankolmogorovarnoldnetworks have been recently introduced as an alternative to traditional MLPs in deep learning applications.}
elements.sent-2411-110: Unlike MLPs, which use fixed activation functions, KANs employ learnable B-spline functions as activation layers, making them more flexible and expressive while requiring fewer parameters.
elements.sent-2411-111: B-splines, composed of polynomial segments with control points, allow local adjustments in the activation function without impacting the rest of the network.
elements.sent-2411-112: This local control helps maintain smoothness and facilitates differentiability, which is crucial for efficient backpropagation.
elements.sent-2411-113: KANs are based on the Kolmogorov-Arnold representation theorem (KART), which states that any continuous high-dimensional function can be decomposed into a sum of univariate functions.
elements.sent-2411-114: Liu et al. \cite{liu2024kankolmogorovarnoldnetworks extended this to deep networks by creating the KAN-Layer, which consists of learnable activation functions given by the equation: \begin{equation}\varphi(x) = w \left( b(x) + \text{spline}(x) \right)\end{equation} where, $b(x) = \text{silu (x) = {x /{(1 + e^{-x )}$ and $spline(x)$ is a linear combination of learnable B-splines.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-115: This structure allows KANs to capture complex functions effectively.
elements.sent-2411-116: The need for adaptable and computationally efficient models has grown recently, especially in domains where fine-tuning large models for downstream tasks can be computationally expensive.
elements.sent-2411-117: Traditional methods like LoRA modules (Section \ref{sec:lora_preliminary ) address this need by introducing learnable parameters in a lower-dimensional space, typically employing MLPs for transformation.}
elements.sent-2411-118: However, MLPs face limitations in efficiently modeling complex non-linear transformations.
elements.sent-2411-119: To overcome these limitations, we propose KAN Adapters, a novel approach leveraging Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) with learnable spline-based activation functions.
elements.sent-2411-120: This architecture efficiently approximates complex, highly non-linear functions, enhancing parameter efficiency and flexibility.
elements.sent-2411-121: Replacing MLP-based LoRA with a KAN adapter improves computational efficiency and adaptability, making it well-suited for tasks requiring sophisticated non-linear representations.
elements.sent-2411-122: The KAN adapter architecture, illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:adapter , incorporates KAN as an adapter layer alongside the pre-trained model's MLP layer.}
elements.sent-2411-123: The KAN adapter layer processes input $X$, which includes down-projection ($\phi_{down}$) and up-projection ($\phi_{up}$) functions.
elements.sent-2411-124: These functions transform the input to a lower-dimensional space and then project it back, producing $X'$ using learnable, spline-based activation functions.
elements.sent-2411-125: The outputs from the MLP and KAN adapters are then aggregated and passed to the subsequent layer.
elements.sent-2411-126: For explanatory purposes, we used the MLP layer to add KAN adapters in parallel; however, similar to LoRA, these adapters can be added to any layer.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.7\columnwidth]{sec/img/adapter.pdf} \caption{Integration of the KAN Adapter with a pre-trained model. Snowflake symbol (\textcolor{blue}{\ding{105}}) indicates frozen weights, and the fire symbol \(\fire\) denotes trainable weights.} \label{fig:adapter}
elements.sent-2411-127: As depicted in Figure \ref{fig:kan_adapter , the input \( X\in\{ x_1, x_2, x_3, \dots, x_m \}\) is projected into a lower-dimensional representation \(H\in\{ h_1, h_2, h_3, \dots, h_n\}\), where \( X\in \mathbb{R ^m\), \( H\in \mathbb{R ^n\), with $n$ = $m/2$, through \begin{equation}\phi_{\text{down}}(X) = W_b^{\text{down}} \cdot g(X) + W_s^{\text{down}} \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i \, \gamma(X) \right)\end{equation} Here, \( W_b^{\text{down \) and \( W_s^{\text{down \) are learnable stabilizing matrices, \( g(X) \) is an activation function, and \( \gamma_i(X) \) represents a set of basis functions (such as B-splines or Radial Basis Functions).}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-128: The terms \( c_i \) are the coefficients learned during training.
elements.sent-2411-129: The transformed representation \( H \) is projected back to the original feature space \( \mathbb{R ^m \).}
elements.sent-2411-130: \begin{equation}\phi_{\text{up}}(X) = W_b^{\text{up}} \cdot g(H) + W_s^{\text{up}} \left( \sum_{i=1}^{m} d_i \, \gamma(H) \right)\end{equation} \( W_b^{\text{up \) and \( W_s^{\text{up \) are additional stabilization matrices, \( d_i \) are learned coefficients, and \( \gamma_i(H) \) is the basis function representation in the compressed space.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-131: This up-projection allows the model to re-expand the representation to the original space while retaining the most relevant features.
elements.sent-2411-132: Finally, outputs from the KAN adapter and MLP are aggregated as \begin{equation}X' = \mathcal{L}_{\theta}(X) + \left( \phi_{\text{up}} \cdot \phi_{\text{down}} \right)(X)\end{equation} $\mathcal{L _{\theta (.)$ is the original pre-trained model.}}
elements.sent-2411-133: The final aggregated output $X'$ is then passed to subsequent layers, ensuring that the KAN adapter's non-linear transformations complement the MLP's capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-134: This complements the main model by introducing non-linear transformations, which LoRA's MLP layers cannot capture efficiently.
elements.sent-2411-135: Salient features: KAN Adapters differ fundamentally from LoRA's fixed MLP-based transformations by utilizing flexible, learnable basis functions.
elements.sent-2411-136: This enables KAN Adapters to capture fine-grained patterns within the data, which is crucial for tasks where complex dependencies must be learned.
elements.sent-2411-137: While LoRA adapters are designed to prevent catastrophic forgetting by keeping the core model weights frozen, KAN Adapters offer an additional layer of resilience through their unique use of adaptive, learnable activation functions.
elements.sent-2411-138: Unlike static activations in MLP-based LoRA, the KAN Adapter's non-linear functions can dynamically adjust to new tasks, selectively retaining important representations from prior tasks without overwriting them.
elements.sent-2411-139: This flexibility enables KAN Adapters to handle the complexities of sequential learning better, as they can modulate responses specifically for new data while preserving critical knowledge from earlier tasks.
elements.sent-2411-140: This added adaptability has been shown in related studies, such as ReLU-KAN \cite{qiu2024relu , to provide enhanced memory retention, making KAN Adapters particularly suited for continual learning scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-141: Additionally, KAN Adapters demonstrate faster convergence compared to LoRA (refer to Figure \ref{fig:model_loss_curve ).}
elements.sent-2411-142: This faster convergence implies that KAN Adapters can reach optimal or near-optimal performance in fewer training steps, making them more computationally efficient.
elements.sent-2411-143: Implementation Details: All models were fine-tuned with a batch size of 12 using gradient accumulation steps of 12, leveraging the AdamW optimizer for enhanced generalization through weight decay.
elements.sent-2411-144: The initial learning rate was set to \(1 \times 10^{-5}\) and dynamically adjusted with a cosine annealing schedule, gradually varying between a minimum of \(1 \times 10^{-7}\) and a maximum of \(2 \times 10^{-3}\).
elements.sent-2411-145: This scheduling approach allowed for smooth convergence by reducing the learning rate as training progressed.
elements.sent-2411-146: The training was conducted on a robust setup of 4 x 8 Nvidia H100 GPUs with 80GB VRAM.
elements.sent-2411-147: For all the baselines, elastic weight consolidation \cite{kirkpatrick2017overcoming was performed to determine where to add LoRA and KAN adapters.}

elements.sent-2411-193: To investigate the impact of different basis functions in the KAN adapter, we consider primarily three types of multivariate function-approximating polynomials: B-Spline, standard Radial Basis Function (RBF), and Gaussian.
elements.sent-2411-194: As shown in Table \ref{tab:ablation , the B-Spline basis achieved an FID of 6.18 and a CLIPSIM score of 0.3002, marking it as a middle-ground performer.}
elements.sent-2411-195: B-splines, being piecewise polynomials, excel at providing localized control over the function, which is helpful for smooth approximations.
elements.sent-2411-196: However, unlike RBFs, they are sensitive to grid-based recalculations and re-scaling, which can limit their stability and efficiency in higher-dimensional applications.
elements.sent-2411-197: In contrast, the standard RBF delivered the lowest FID (6.09) and a strong CLIPSIM of 0.3276, achieving the best balance between sample realism and representational consistency.
elements.sent-2411-198: Unlike Gaussian RBFs, which rely on a fixed decay rate, standard RBFs are not bound to a specific decay, allowing them to adapt to complex patterns in data flexibly.
elements.sent-2411-199: This flexibility may explain its superior FID, as it maintains realistic details more effectively than Gaussian and B-Spline functions.
elements.sent-2411-200: While slightly higher in FID (6.33), the Gaussian basis recorded the highest CLIPSIM at 0.3301, suggesting that it prioritizes structural consistency over fine-grained details.
elements.sent-2411-201: Gaussian functions have smooth and symmetrical decay properties, promoting stability in feature representation but at the cost of adaptability.
elements.sent-2411-202: This fixed decay makes it less effective at capturing localized detail than the general RBF, as seen in the higher FID score.

elements.section-2411-008: Fine-Tuning Template for Outfit Sketch Generation
elements.sent-2411-208: We developed a structured prompt template for fine-tuning the models.
elements.sent-2411-209: This template was designed to capture essential aspects of each outfit sketch, including model presence, gender, pose, outfit description, color details and accessories.
elements.sent-2411-210: By standardizing these attributes, we aimed to provide the model with clear, detailed inputs that could guide the generation process toward accurate, high-quality fashion sketches.
elements.sent-2411-211: The template used is as follows:.
elements.verbatim-2411-001: MODEL PRESENCE: {MODEL_PRESENCE} MODEL GENDER: {MODEL_GENDER} MODEL POSE: {MODEL_POSE} OUTFIT DESCRIPTION: {OUTFIT_DESCRIPTION} COLOR DETAILS: {COLOR_DETAILS} ACCESSORIES: {ACCESSORIES}
elements.section-2411-009: Detailed Description of Human Evaluation Metrics
elements.sent-2411-212: For a thorough evaluation of the generated fashion sketches, we defined seven key metrics, each capturing specific aspects of image quality and alignment with the prompt.
elements.sent-2411-213: The main paper presents human evaluation results (refer to Table \ref{tab:human_eval ).}
elements.sent-2411-214: These metrics allowed us to perform a comprehensive assessment, ensuring that both technical quality and alignment with the prompt are rigorously evaluated:.
elements.sent-2411-215: Prompt Relevance: Evaluates how accurately the generated image reflects the essential elements described in the prompt.
elements.sent-2411-216: This metric focuses on effectively visualizing the main idea or theme specified.
elements.sent-2411-217: Visual Quality: Assesses the technical quality of the image, including factors such as resolution, sharpness, clarity, and the overall visual appeal.
elements.sent-2411-218: Creativity: Measures the degree of innovation and imaginative interpretation in the image.
elements.sent-2411-219: This metric considers how the generated image demonstrates creativity while staying coherent with the prompt's details.
elements.sent-2411-220: Pose and Model Accuracy: Examines the alignment between the model's physical representation in the image, such as pose, gender, and stance and the specifications given in the prompt.
elements.sent-2411-221: Color Palette Alignment: Compares the colors used in the generated image to the colors described in the prompt, assessing accuracy in terms of color scheme and consistency.
elements.sent-2411-222: Contextual Fit: Evaluates how well the image fits the broader context implied by the prompt, including aspects like mood, setting, and style.
elements.sent-2411-223: Overall Appeal: Provides a holistic assessment of the image's attractiveness and visual impact, taking into account its general appeal to viewers.
elements.sent-2411-224: To create the FLORA dataset, we employed a multi-stage filtering and description process to ensure that the images met quality standards and contained only the desired fashion elements.
elements.sent-2411-225: Below, we describe the stages of filtering, noise removal, and detailed description generation, along with the prompts used at each step.
elements.sent-2411-226: Initial Filtering of Noise Using VLM Model: In the initial filtering stage, we used a VLM model (LLaVa 32b) to identify and remove images that did not meet the criteria for inclusion in the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-227: Specifically, we aimed to retain images that contained a single outfit sketch without extraneous objects.
elements.sent-2411-228: To achieve this, we provided each image with the following prompts and used the model's responses to segregate the data: \begin{promptbox Prompt 1: ``Is there a single outfit present in the image? Yes or No?" Prompt 2: ``Is the outfit present in the image a sketch? Yes or No?" Prompt 3: ``Does the image only contain the sketch without any other objects? Yes or No?" Prompt 4: ``Are there multiple outfits present in the image? Yes or No?" \end{promptbox If the VLLM model answered ``Yes" to the questions indicating the desired characteristics (single outfit, sketch format, no additional objects, and no multiple outfits), the image proceeded to the next filtering stage.}}
elements.sent-2411-229: If the answer to any of these prompts was "No," the image was discarded.
elements.sent-2411-230: Watermark Detection and Removal: For images that passed the initial filtering, we implemented an additional check to detect and remove watermarks, which could interfere with model training.
elements.sent-2411-231: At this stage as well we utilized the LLaVa 32b with the following prompt: \begin{promptbox Prompt: ``Does this image contain a watermark? A watermark may appear as text or a logo superimposed over the image.}
elements.sent-2411-232: Yes or No" \end{promptbox If the model identified a watermark ("Yes" response), we performed image inpainting to remove the watermark, as described in the main paper.}
elements.sent-2411-233: This ensured that the images in the dataset were clean and free from visual obstructions.
elements.sent-2411-234: Input Prompt Generation: Once the images were filtered, we used GPT-4o to generate precise and comprehensive descriptions of each outfit.
elements.sent-2411-235: This process involved a multi-turn approach, where each turn focused on describing a specific aspect of the image.
elements.sent-2411-236: The following prompts were used to extract detailed information: \begin{promptbox Model Pose Description: ``Describe the model's posture.}
elements.sent-2411-237: Is the model standing, sitting, walking, or in any other specific pose? Detail the position of the model's hands, legs, and overall body stance.
elements.sent-2411-238: Provide enough detail so that someone reading the description could recreate the sketch with high accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-239: Note: Generate a response in a single paragraph with not more than 50 words.
elements.sent-2411-240: Please note that - while describing pose, do not describe the dress the model is wearing or do not use any adjectives.
elements.sent-2411-241: Just in simple words, explain the pose of the model, nothing else." \end{promptbox \begin{promptbox Outfit Description: ``Describe the outfit present in the image.}}
elements.sent-2411-242: Describe in a way that a fashion designer can read that description and create an exact similar sketch of the outfit.
elements.sent-2411-244: Describe garment type, fabric, cut, and any special features.
elements.sent-2411-245: Note: Generate a response in a single paragraph with not more than 200 words." \end{promptbox \begin{promptbox Color Details: ``Provide a detailed description of the color scheme used in the outfit.}}
elements.sent-2411-246: Mention the primary color, any secondary colors, and any unique color patterns.
elements.sent-2411-247: Note: Generate the response in a single paragraph with not more than 50 words.
elements.sent-2411-248: Just return color-related information." \end{promptbox \begin{promptbox Accessories Description: ``Mention and describe any accessories or additional design elements included in the sketch, such as belts, shoes, jewelry, or headpieces.}}
elements.sent-2411-249: If no accessories are present, respond with `no additional accessories present'." \end{promptbox This multi-turn approach allowed us to capture specific details of each image, resulting in a dataset that accurately reflects the visual and stylistic characteristics required for high-quality, fashion-focused AI training.}
elements.sent-2411-250: Additionally, we extracted information on the presence and gender of the model in each image using LLaVa 32b.
elements.sent-2411-251: By using this layered approach - initial filtering, watermark detection, and detailed multi-turn descriptions; we created a high-quality, fashion-specific dataset (FLORA) that supports the generation of accurate and stylistically rich images from textual descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-252: This structured dataset creation process ensures that the FLORA dataset is diverse, precise, and highly applicable to fashion-focused generative AI tasks.

elements.sent-2411-269: In this section, we investigate the use of KAN adapters as an alternative to LoRA.
elements.sent-2411-270: Extending our previous ablation studies, we explore the effect of varying ranks for LoRA and downsample rates for KAN adapters.
elements.sent-2411-271: For this task, we choose FLUX as our baseline model for conducting the experiments.
elements.sent-2411-272: Table \ref{tab:ablation_lora_kan provides the CLIP-SIM and FID scores for different ranks and downsampling rates for the respective adapters.}
elements.sent-2411-273: Effect of Rank on LoRA Performance: For LoRA, we observe a progressive improvement in both CLIP-SIM and FID scores as the rank increases.
elements.sent-2411-274: The model achieves the highest performance at rank $r=64$, yielding a CLIP-SIM of 0.2987 and an FID of 7.88.

elements.sent-2411-276: Effect of Downsample Rate on KAN Performance: The downsample rate in the KAN Adapter refers to the dimensionality reduction applied to the input features.
elements.sent-2411-277: Specifically, as explained in section \ref{sec:KANadapter , the KAN adapter incorporates two main functions: the $\phi_{down}$ and $\phi_{up}$.}
elements.sent-2411-278: Here, $n$ is the downsampling rate, which is hyper-parameter and is decided by the ratio by which we want to reduce dimension (e.g. $m/16$, $m/8$, $m/4$, $m/2$).
elements.sent-2411-279: Where a smaller downsample rate ($m/16$) implies greater reduction and larger rates ($m/2$) retain more of the original feature space.
elements.sent-2411-280: The ablation study, as shown in the table \ref{tab:ablation_lora_kan , evaluates the performance of KAN Adapters at different downsample rates.}
elements.sent-2411-281: As the downsampling rate increases from $m/16$ to $m/2$ results in improvements in both CLIP-SIM and FID scores.
elements.sent-2411-282: This progression highlights the trade-off between dimensionality reduction and feature retention, with higher downsample rates striking an optimal balance between computational efficiency and model performance.
elements.sent-2411-283: These findings emphasize the importance of selecting an appropriate downsample rate to maximize the KAN Adapter's effectiveness.

elements.sent-2411-304: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) \cite{hu2021lora is a parameter-efficient fine-tuning method that enables large pre-trained models to adapt to new tasks while keeping most parameters frozen.}
elements.sent-2411-305: Instead of updating the full model weights, LoRA decomposes weight updates into low-rank matrices, significantly reducing the number of trainable parameters and memory overhead.
elements.sent-2411-306: Unlike architectural modifications, LoRA does not alter the model’s structure but instead modifies weight updates in a low-dimensional space.
elements.sent-2411-307: Mathematically, for a weight matrix \( W \in \mathbb{R ^{d \times d \), LoRA approximates its update \( \Delta W \) as: \begin{equation}\Delta W = W_{\text{up}} W_{\text{down}}\end{equation} where \( W_{\text{up \in \mathbb{R ^{d \times l \) and \( W_{\text{down \in \mathbb{R ^{l \times d \), with \( l \ll d \).}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-308: The low-rank decomposition ensures efficient adaptation without significantly increasing computational costs.

elements.sent-2411-310: As depicted in Figure \ref{fig:adapter , the input \( X\in\{ x_1, x_2, x_3, \dots, x_m \}\) is projected into a lower-dimensional representation \(H\in\{ h_1, h_2, h_3, \dots, h_n\}\), where \( X\in \mathbb{R ^m\), \( H\in \mathbb{R ^n\), with $n$ = $m/2$, through \begin{equation}\phi_{\text{down}}(X) = W_b^{\text{down}} \cdot g(X) + W_s^{\text{down}} \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} c_i \, \gamma(X) \right)\end{equation} Here, \( W_b^{\text{down \) and \( W_s^{\text{down \) are learnable stabilizing matrices, \( g(X) \) is an activation function, and \( \gamma_i(X) \) represents a set of basis functions (such as B-splines or Radial Basis Functions).}}}}}}}

elements.section-2411-011: KAN Adapters vs. LoRA: A Mathematical Justification
elements.sent-2411-338: This section provides mathematical proof demonstrating why Kormologolav–Arnold Network (KAN) adapters are strictly more expressive than Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) adapters.
elements.sent-2411-339: We begin by formally defining LoRA and KAN, followed by a Taylor series-based proof demonstrating why KAN provides a superior function approximation.
elements.sent-2411-340: Finally, we connect KAN’s capabilities with the Kolmogorov–Arnold Representation Theorem to reinforce its universal approximation ability.
elements.sent-2411-341: LoRA - A Linear, Low-Rank Adaptation: LoRA modifies a model by adding a low-rank update $\Delta W$ to an existing frozen weight matrix $W_0$.
elements.sent-2411-342: Mathematically, this is defined as: \begin{equation}W = W_0 + \Delta W, \quad \Delta W = W_{\text{up}} \, W_{\text{down}}\end{equation} where:.
elements.sent-2411-343: Given an input $x \in \mathbb{R ^d$, the transformed output is: \begin{equation}\text{LoRA-Output} = W_0 \, x + W_{\text{up}} \,(W_{\text{down}} \, x)\end{equation} This transformation remains purely linear and is restricted to rank $r$, meaning LoRA cannot capture nonlinear interactions between features.}
elements.sent-2411-344: KAN Adapter - A More Expressive Nonlinear Adapter: KAN adapter builds on LoRA but introduces basis function expansions that allow for nonlinear transformations.
elements.sent-2411-345: The key difference is that KAN adapter consists of:.
elements.sent-2411-346: A down-projection, which maps input features into a transformed space using nonlinear functions and basis expansions.
elements.sent-2411-347: An up-projection, which reconstructs an adapted version of the input.
elements.sent-2411-348: Mathematically, this is defined as: \begin{equation}H = \phi_{\text{down}}(X) \;=\; W_b^{\text{down}} \; g(X) \;+\; W_s^{\text{down}} \sum_{i=1}^n c_i \,\gamma_i(X),\end{equation} \begin{equation}X'_{\text{adapter}} = \phi_{\text{up}}(H) \;=\; W_b^{\text{up}} \; g(H) \;+\; W_s^{\text{up}} \sum_{j=1}^m d_j \,\gamma_j(H),\end{equation} where:.
elements.sent-2411-349: Since KAN adapter explicitly models nonlinear transformations, it can capture complex relationships between features, unlike LoRA.
elements.sent-2411-350: A key property of KAN adapter is its ability to approximate arbitrary smooth functions, which LoRA cannot do.
elements.sent-2411-351: We prove this using Taylor series expansions.
elements.sent-2411-352: Step 1: Approximating Functions with Taylor Series For any sufficiently smooth function $f: \mathbb{R ^d \to \mathbb{R ^d$ a Taylor series expansion around $x=0$ gives: \begin{equation}\begin{split} f_k(x) = \underbrace{f_k(0)}_{\text{constant term}} \;+\; \sum_{\alpha} \frac{1}{\alpha!} \left(\frac{\partial^{|\alpha|} f_k}{\partial x^\alpha}\Bigl\vert_{x=0}\right) x^\alpha + \text{(higher-order remainder)} \end{split}\end{equation} where,.}}
elements.formula-2411-011: \begin{equation} f_k(x) = \underbrace{f_k(0)}_{constant term} \;+\; \sum_{\alpha} \frac{1}{\alpha!} \left(\frac{\partial^{|\alpha|} f_k}{\partial x^\alpha}\Bigl\vert_{x=0}\right) x^\alpha + (higher-order remainder) \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-353: Truncating this expansion at order $L$ provides a polynomial approximation: \begin{equation}f(x) \approx P_L(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{N_L} c_i \,\gamma_i(x),\end{equation} where, $\gamma_i(x)$ are monomials up to degree $L$.
elements.sent-2411-354: Since KAN adapter incorporates these basis functions explicitly, it can approximate any smooth function arbitrarily well—something LoRA cannot achieve with its strictly linear transformations.
elements.sent-2411-355: Step 2: How KAN adapter Uses Taylor Expansions for Adaptation In a KAN adapter, let the down-projection be: \begin{equation}H \;=\; W_b^{\text{down}} \, g(x) \;+\; W_s^{\text{down}} \sum_{i=1}^{N_L} c_i \,\gamma_i(x),\end{equation} where,.
elements.sent-2411-356: Then let the up-projection be: \begin{equation}X'_{\text{adapter}} \;=\; W_b^{\text{up}} \, g(H) \;+\; W_s^{\text{up}} \sum_{j=1}^m d_j \,\gamma_j(H).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-357: Now $X'_{\text{adapter }$ is (in principle) a polynomial composition in $x$: \begin{equation}X'_{\text{adapter}} \;=\; W_b^{\text{up}} \, g\Bigl(W_b^{\text{down}} g(x) + \ldots\Bigr) \;+\; W_s^{\text{up}} \sum_{j=1}^m d_j \,\gamma_j\Bigl(H\Bigr).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-358: Expanding $\gamma_j\bigl(H\bigr)$ yields a polynomial in the entries of $H$, each of which is itself a polynomial (plus nonlinearity $g$) in $x$.
elements.sent-2411-359: Provided $g$ is a smooth or well-behaved activation, $X'_{\text{adapter }$ can approximate a wide class of continuous functions $\mathbb{R ^d \to \mathbb{R ^d$, surpassing strictly linear transformations.}}}
elements.sent-2411-360: Step 3: Universality Argument (Local Approximation) We now formalize the fact that KAN adapters can approximate any smooth function locally, while LoRA is limited to low-rank transformations.
elements.sent-2411-361: Theorem (Polynomial Approximation): Let $f: \mathbb{R ^d \to \mathbb{R ^d$ be analytic in a neighborhood of $0$.}}
elements.sent-2411-362: Then for any $\epsilon > 0$, there exists a polynomial $P_L(x)$ of finite degree $L$ such that, \begin{equation}\|\,f(x) - P_L(x)\| \;<\; \epsilon\end{equation} for all $x$ in some sufficiently small region.
elements.sent-2411-363: This means that by choosing sufficiently many polynomial terms, KAN adapter’s basis expansions can approximate any smooth function arbitrarily well within a local region.
elements.sent-2411-364: LoRA, however, is constrained to: \begin{equation}f_{\text{LoRA}}(x) = W_{\text{up}} \,(W_{\text{down}} \, x) + \text{constant terms},\end{equation} which is a $rank-r$ linear map in $x$ and cannot capture higher-order (nonlinear) dependencies.
elements.sent-2411-365: This formally proves that KAN adapter is strictly more expressive than LoRA in local function approximation.
elements.sent-2411-366: Step 4: Kolmogorov–Arnold Representation Theorem Perspective While the Taylor series argument above shows local universal approximation, the classical Kolmogorov--Arnold Representation Theorem states that any multivariate continuous function on a compact domain can be written as a sum and composition of univariate continuous functions.
elements.sent-2411-367: Formally, in one version: Kolmogorov--Arnold Representation: For any continuous function \begin{equation}F : [0,1]^d \;\to\; \mathbb{R},\end{equation} there exist univariate continuous functions $\phi_q, \psi_{qk}$ such that \begin{equation}F(x_1, \dots, x_d) = \sum_{q=0}^{2d} \phi_q\Bigl( \sum_{k=1}^d \psi_{qk}(x_k) \Bigr).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-368: This theorem implies that if a network architecture can realize generic compositions of univariate basis expansions, it can approximate any continuous multivariate function on a cube $[0,1]^d$.
elements.sent-2411-369: KAN-style adapters, which rely on basis expansions and compositions ($\phi_{\text{down }$, $\phi_{\text{up }$), inherently align with this representation principle.}}
elements.sent-2411-370: Thus, by Kolmogorov--Arnold, KAN adapter can approximate any continuous function, whereas LoRA’s linear approach cannot achieve such universality in higher dimensions.
elements.subsection-2411-016: Why KAN adapter Is Strictly More Expressive than LoRA
elements.sent-2411-371: LoRA as a Special (Linear) Case of KAN adapter: If we disable the basis expansions and nonlinearities in KAN adapter: \begin{equation}W_s^{\text{down}} = 0,\quad W_s^{\text{up}} = 0,\quad g(x) = x,\end{equation} then \begin{equation}\phi_{\text{down}}(x) = W_b^{\text{down}}\,x, \quad \phi_{\text{up}}(H) = W_b^{\text{up}}\,H,\end{equation} giving \begin{equation}X'_{\text{adapter}} = W_b^{\text{up}}\,\bigl(W_b^{\text{down}} x\bigr),\end{equation} which is precisely a rank-$n$ linear map akin to LoRA.
elements.sent-2411-372: Thus, LoRA is embedded in KAN adapter as a degenerate (linear-only) configuration.
elements.sent-2411-373: Nonlinear and Higher-Order Terms in KAN adapter: In general, KAN adapter includes expansions: \begin{equation}\sum_{i} c_i \gamma_i(x), \quad \gamma_i(x) = \text{(e.g.\ polynomial monomial)}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-374: Hence, $X'_{\text{adapter }$ can contain terms of the form $x_1^{\alpha_1 x_2^{\alpha_2 \cdots x_d^{\alpha_d}$ or other advanced expansions (Fourier, spline, etc.).}}}
elements.sent-2411-375: No purely linear LoRA block can replicate such higher-order interactions without artificially stacking multiple linear LoRA blocks across many layers.
elements.sent-2411-376: Taylor-series-based argument: By leveraging polynomial expansions up to order $L$, KAN adapter can approximate local behavior of smooth functions to arbitrary precision.
elements.sent-2411-377: LoRA, being linear and low-rank, lacks this capacity.
elements.sent-2411-378: Kolmogorov--Arnold-based argument: KAN adapter’s composition of univariate basis expansions aligns with the Kolmogorov--Arnold Theorem, thus enabling universal approximation for continuous functions on compact domains in $\mathbb{R ^d$.}
elements.sent-2411-379: LoRA is a special case: Setting all basis expansions to zero and removing nonlinearities recovers a linear rank-$r$ form, demonstrating that KAN adapter strictly generalizes LoRA.
elements.sent-2411-380: Therefore, KAN adapters can realize richer, higher-order function approximations, surpassing the purely linear rank-$r$ approach of LoRA.
elements.sent-2411-381: Consequently, KAN adapter works better than LoRA in scenarios demanding expressive, nonlinear transformations while retaining the efficient, adapter-based paradigm.
elements.sent-2411-382: In practice, a KAN adapter may incorporate polynomial or spline expansions of a modest order, often capturing crucial nonlinearities with fewer parameters than a naive full-rank update.
elements.sent-2411-383: Empirical results generally confirm that KAN adapter-like architectures can adapt large models more flexibly than LoRA alone, especially under distribution shifts or tasks requiring higher-order feature interactions.

elements.sent-2411-384: As a second orthogonal contribution, we introduce NeRA (Nonlinear low-rank Expressive Representation Adapter), a novel adapter architecture based on Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KAN).
elements.sent-2411-385: Unlike traditional PEFT techniques such as LoRA, LoKR, DoRA, and LoHA that use MLP adapters, NeRA uses learnable spline-based nonlinear transformations, enabling superior modeling of complex semantic relationships, achieving strong fidelity, faster convergence and semantic alignment.
elements.sent-2411-386: Extensive experiments on our proposed FLORA and LAION-5B datasets validate the superiority of NeRA over existing adapters.
elements.sent-2411-387: We will open-source both the FLORA dataset and our implementation code.
elements.sent-2411-388: The global fashion industry, valued at $2.4$ trillion, has been growing at an annual rate of $5.5\%$ over the past decade \cite{mckinsey2022stateoffashion .}
elements.sent-2411-389: Major fashion brands are leveraging AI to enhance creativity, efficiency and consumer personalization \cite{fashionai1, fashionai2 .}
elements.sent-2411-390: AI's role in fashion ranges from virtual try-on systems to trend forecasting from social media and fashion show datasets, product development, supply chain optimization, and sustainable practices \cite{sustfashion .}
elements.sent-2411-391: For instance, datasets like FLORA present unique challenges due to their emphasis on fine-grained details such as fabric type, fit, and decorative elements which require nuanced understanding and multi-modal alignment.
elements.sent-2411-392: These subtle attributes introduce high variability and complexity, making it difficult for models to generalize well.
elements.sent-2411-393: To address this, we propose NeRA (Nonlinear low-rank Expressive Representation Adapter), a new adapter architecture designed to capture fine-grained, nonlinear dependencies in complex datasets like FLORA.
elements.sent-2411-394: NeRA leverages spline-based activations inspired by Kolmogorov–Arnold Networks to enable expressive and flexible feature transformation, improving ability to capture complex, fine-grained relationships.
elements.sent-2411-395: We make the following key contributions:.
elements.sent-2411-396: FLORA will allow AI models to create precise, high-quality fashion sketches from text, easing reliance on manual sketching and accelerating early design phases.
elements.sent-2411-397: NeRA Adapter: We propose NeRA, a nonlinear low-rank adapter architecture based on Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs)~\cite{liu2024kankolmogorovarnoldnetworks .}
elements.sent-2411-398: By leveraging learnable spline-based activations, NeRA enables more expressive and flexible feature transformations than MLP-based adapters such as LoRA, LoKR, DoRA, and LoHA, improving semantic alignment and fidelity in generative tasks.
elements.sent-2411-399: Its parameter efficiency allows large diffusion models to adapt to fine-grained tasks like fashion sketch synthesis.
elements.sent-2411-400: Experiments show that NeRA achieves faster convergence and higher fidelity than MLP based adapters, enabling models to reach optimal performance in fewer training steps.

elements.sent-2411-404: Transformer based text-to-image models, such as DALL-E \cite{ramesh2021zeroshottexttoimagegeneration , Imagen \cite{saharia2022photorealistictexttoimagediffusionmodels , and Stable Diffusion \cite{Stable-Diffusion-1.5, Stable-Diffusion-3 now enable AI to create garment visuals from descriptions.}}}
elements.sent-2411-405: Recent datasets like Market-1501 \cite{Zheng_2015_ICCV , DeepFashion \cite{liuLQWTcvpr16DeepFashion , and VITON \cite{han2017viton support fashion synthesis applications such as human parsing, pose estimation, landmark detection, style transfer, and clothing simulation.}}}
elements.sent-2411-406: DeepFashion and DeepFashion2 \cite{ge2019deepfashion2 have enabled tasks such as garment recognition, landmark detection, and segmentation by providing high-quality annotations of real-world fashion images.}
elements.sent-2411-407: FashionGen \cite{rostamzadeh2018fashion contributed richly annotated catalog images with class labels suitable for GAN-based generation, while DeepFashion-Multimodal \cite{jiang2022text2human further incorporated textual descriptions to support cross-modal retrieval and generation.}}
elements.sent-2411-408: VITON-HD \cite{choi2021viton has been pivotal for virtual try-on tasks, offering paired images of individuals in different outfits.}
elements.sent-2411-409: However, these too are designed for virtual try-on synthesis and contain studio-quality model images rather than conceptual design sketches.
elements.sent-2411-410: Likewise, the Fashion Synthesis dataset introduced in the same work supports latent diffusion-based outfit generation but still remains grounded in posed model photography rather than design-centric imagery.
elements.sent-2411-411: Despite their utility, all of these datasets rely on photographic images and lack the abstraction and creative intent represented in designer sketches.
elements.sent-2411-412: To the best of our knowledge, no existing dataset includes detailed, text-paired fashion sketches an essential modality in the early phases of fashion design.
elements.sent-2411-413: FLORA is the first such dataset, comprising high-quality sketch-text pairs with rich, domain-specific descriptions that reflect the professional vocabulary and compositional detail found in designer workflows.
elements.sent-2411-414: Related work on Parameter Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT) Method: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) \cite{hu2021lora enables efficient fine-tuning of large models.}
elements.sent-2411-415: Instead of updating all model parameters, LoRA freezes the original model weights and introduces a pair of trainable low-rank matrices.
elements.sent-2411-416: Formally, for a weight matrix \( W \in \mathbb{R ^{d \times d \), the update \( \Delta W \) is expressed as: \begin{equation}\Delta W = W_{\text{up}} W_{\text{down}}\end{equation} where \( W_{\text{up \in \mathbb{R ^{d \times l \) and \( W_{\text{down \in \mathbb{R ^{l \times d \), with \( l \ll d \), minimizing computational and memory overhead.}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-430: As a second orthogonal contribution, we propose NeRA (Nonlinear low-rank Expressive Representation Adapter).
elements.sent-2411-431: Traditional methods like LoRA modules address this need by introducing learnable parameters in a lower-dimensional space, typically employing MLPs for transformation.
elements.sent-2411-432: Our proposed NeRA overcomes these limitations.
elements.sent-2411-433: It leverages KANs with learnable spline-based activation functions.
elements.sent-2411-434: Replacing MLP-based LoRA with NeRA improves computational efficiency and adaptability, making it well-suited for tasks requiring sophisticated non-linear representations.
elements.sent-2411-435: We first provide an overview on KAN modules, and then discuss our proposed NeRA adapter.
elements.sent-2411-436: Background on KANs: KANs \cite{liu2024kankolmogorovarnoldnetworks replace traditional MLP activations with learnable B-spline functions, offering greater flexibility and expressiveness with fewer parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-437: B-splines allow localized adjustments while preserving smoothness and differentiability for efficient backpropagation.
elements.sent-2411-438: Based on the Kolmogorov-Arnold representation theorem, any high-dimensional function can be expressed as a sum of univariate functions.
elements.sent-2411-439: In the KAN layer \cite{liu2024kankolmogorovarnoldnetworks , activation is defined as: \begin{equation}\varphi(x) = w \left( b(x) + \text{spline}(x) \right)\end{equation} Here, $b(x) = \text{silu (x) = {x /{(1 + e^{-x )}$ and $spline(x)$ is a learnable linear combination of B-splines, enabling KANs to effectively model complex functions.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-440: Proposed NeRA Adapter: NeRA architecture (Figure \ref{fig:adapter ) incorporates KAN as an adapter layer alongside the pre-trained model's MLP layer.}
elements.sent-2411-441: Similar to LoRA, these adapters can be added to any layer.
elements.sent-2411-442: The NeRA adapter layer processes input $X$, which includes down-projection ($\phi_{down}$) and up-projection ($\phi_{up}$) functions.
elements.sent-2411-443: Then, MLP and NeRA adapter outputs are aggregated and passed to the subsequent layer.
elements.figure-2411-045: \includegraphics[width=0.6\columnwidth]{nera.pdf} \caption{Integration of the NeRA with a pre-trained model. \textcolor{blue}{\ding{105}} and \(\fire\) indicate frozen and trainable weights, respectively. } \label{fig:adapter}
elements.sent-2411-444: As depicted in Figure \ref{fig:adapter , the input \( X\in\{ x_1, x_2, x_3, \dots, x_m \}\) is projected into a lower-dimensional representation \(H\in\{ h_1, h_2, h_3, \dots, h_n\}\), where \( X\in \mathbb{R ^m\), \( H\in \mathbb{R ^n\), with $n$ = $m/2$, through \begin{equation}\phi_{\text{down}}(X) = W_b^{\text{down}} \cdot g(X) + W_s^{\text{down}} \left( \sum\nolimits_{i=1}^{n} c_i \, \gamma(X) \right)\end{equation} Here, \( W_b^{\text{down \) and \( W_s^{\text{down \) are learnable stabilizing matrices, \( g(X) \) is an activation function, and \( \gamma_i(X) \) represents a set of basis functions (such as B-splines or Radial Basis Functions).}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-445: The transformed representation \( H \) is projected back to the original feature space \( \mathbb{R ^m \). \begin{equation}\phi_{\text{up}}(X) = W_b^{\text{up}} \cdot g(H) + W_s^{\text{up}} \left( \sum\nolimits_{i=1}^{m} d_i \, \gamma(H) \right)\end{equation} \( W_b^{\text{up \) and \( W_s^{\text{up \) are additional stabilization matrices, \( d_i \) are learned coefficients, and \( \gamma_i(H) \) is the basis function representation in the compressed space.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-446: Finally, outputs from the NeRA and MLP are aggregated as \begin{equation}X' = \mathcal{L}_{\theta}(X) + \left( \phi_{\text{up}} \cdot \phi_{\text{down}} \right)(X)\end{equation} $\mathcal{L _{\theta (.)$ is the original pre-trained model.}}
elements.sent-2411-447: The final aggregated output $X'$ is then passed to subsequent layers, ensuring that the NeRA's non-linear transformations complement the MLP's capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-448: This complements the main model by introducing non-linear transformations, which traditional PEFT methods like LoRA’s MLP layers struggle to capture efficiently.

elements.sent-2411-449: Salient features: NeRA differs fundamentally from LoRA's fixed MLP-based transformations by utilizing flexible, learnable basis functions.
elements.sent-2411-450: This enables NeRA to capture fine-grained patterns within the data, which is crucial for tasks where complex dependencies must be learned.
elements.sent-2411-451: While LoRA adapters are designed to prevent catastrophic forgetting by keeping the core model weights frozen, NeRA offer an additional layer of resilience through their unique use of adaptive, learnable activation functions.

elements.sent-2411-498: The performance gap stems not from the superiority of general-purpose models, but from differences in training data.
elements.sent-2411-499: Fashion-specific models like FAME-ViL and UniFashion are trained on structured, photorealistic images and catalog-style text, making them less effective on abstract, stylized sketches.
elements.sent-2411-500: They often miss sparse or symbolic cues and fine design details.
elements.sent-2411-501: In contrast, GPT-4o’s diverse, multimodal training enables better generalization to sketch-based tasks, handling semantic ambiguity and visual nuance more effectively.Ultimately, this experiment reveals a key mismatch between the training data of current fashion-specific models and the abstract nature of design sketches, emphasizing the need for more diverse and representative data in fashion model training.
elements.sent-2411-502: We introduce FLORA, the first dataset specifically designed for generating fashion outfits from textual descriptions, alongside NeRA Adapters, a novel architecture leveraging KANs to enhance model adaptability in complex domains.
elements.sent-2411-503: These contributions address two major challenges in AI-driven fashion design: the scarcity of high-quality, semantically rich datasets and the limitations of current techniques in handling fine-grained design control and adaptability.
elements.sent-2411-504: FLORA lays the groundwork for a new generation of generative models capable of interpreting and translating nuanced design prompts into coherent fashion outfit sketches.
elements.sent-2411-505: Looking ahead, we aim to enhance FLORA with part-level sketch editing, enabling models to modify specific outfit elements while maintaining overall style, offering designers greater control and flexibility.
elements.sent-2411-506: We plan to collaborate with industry to broaden the dataset’s diversity.
elements.sent-2411-507: NeRA Adapters, with their nonlinear spline-based design, show strong domain adaptability with modest computational overhead.
elements.sent-2411-508: Future work will focus on optimizing NeRA for efficient, large-scale deployment in real-world fashion applications.

elements.sent-2411-526: Comparison with Existing Personalization Methods: To assess the strengths of our adapter-based fine-tuning approach, we compare it against two widely used personalization techniques - DreamBooth \cite{ruiz2023dreambooth and Textual Inversion \cite{gal2022image each representing different ends of the efficiency fidelity trade-off.}}

elements.section-2411-016: NeRA vs. LoRA: A Mathematical Justification
elements.sent-2411-535: This section provides mathematical proof demonstrating why Nonlinear low-rank Expressive Representation Adapters (NeRA) are strictly more expressive than Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) adapters.
elements.sent-2411-536: We begin by formally defining LoRA and NeRA, followed by a Taylor series-based proof demonstrating why NeRA provides a superior function approximation.
elements.sent-2411-537: Finally, we connect NeRA’s capabilities with the Kolmogorov–Arnold Representation Theorem to reinforce its universal approximation ability.
elements.sent-2411-538: NeRA Adapter - A More Expressive Nonlinear Adapter: NeRA adapter builds on LoRA but introduces basis function expansions that allow for nonlinear transformations.
elements.sent-2411-539: The key difference is that NeRA adapter consists of:.
elements.sent-2411-540: Since NeRA adapter explicitly models nonlinear transformations, it can capture complex relationships between features, unlike LoRA.
elements.sent-2411-541: A key property of NeRA adapter is its ability to approximate arbitrary smooth functions, which LoRA cannot do.
elements.sent-2411-542: Since NeRA adapter incorporates these basis functions explicitly, it can approximate any smooth function arbitrarily well—something LoRA cannot achieve with its strictly linear transformations.

elements.subsection-2411-020: Why NeRA adapter Is Strictly More Expressive than LoRA
elements.sent-2411-549: LoRA as a Special (Linear) Case of NeRA adapter: If we disable the basis expansions and nonlinearities in NeRA adapter: \begin{equation}W_s^{\text{down}} = 0,\quad W_s^{\text{up}} = 0,\quad g(x) = x,\end{equation} then \begin{equation}\phi_{\text{down}}(x) = W_b^{\text{down}}\,x, \quad \phi_{\text{up}}(H) = W_b^{\text{up}}\,H,\end{equation} giving \begin{equation}X'_{\text{adapter}} = W_b^{\text{up}}\,\bigl(W_b^{\text{down}} x\bigr),\end{equation} which is precisely a rank-$n$ linear map akin to LoRA.
elements.sent-2411-550: Thus, LoRA is embedded in NeRA adapter as a degenerate (linear-only) configuration.
elements.sent-2411-551: Nonlinear and Higher-Order Terms in NeRA adapter: In general, NeRA adapter includes expansions: \begin{equation}\sum_{i} c_i \gamma_i(x), \quad \gamma_i(x) = \text{(e.g.\ polynomial monomial)}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-552: Taylor-series-based argument: By leveraging polynomial expansions up to order $L$, NeRA can approximate local behavior of smooth functions to arbitrary precision.
elements.sent-2411-553: Kolmogorov--Arnold-based argument: NeRA’s composition of univariate basis expansions aligns with the Kolmogorov-Arnold Theorem, thus enabling universal approximation for continuous functions on compact domains in $\mathbb{R ^d$.}
elements.sent-2411-554: LoRA is a special case: Setting all basis expansions to zero and removing nonlinearities recovers a linear rank-$r$ form, demonstrating that NeRA strictly generalizes LoRA.
elements.sent-2411-555: Therefore, NeRA can realize richer, higher-order function approximations, surpassing the purely linear rank-$r$ approach of LoRA.
elements.sent-2411-556: Consequently, NeRA adapter works better than LoRA in scenarios demanding expressive, nonlinear transformations while retaining the efficient, adapter-based paradigm.
elements.sent-2411-557: In practice, a NeRA may incorporate polynomial or spline expansions of a modest order, often capturing crucial nonlinearities with fewer parameters than a naive full-rank update.
elements.sent-2411-558: Empirical results generally confirm that NeRA-like architectures can adapt large models more flexibly than LoRA alone, especially under distribution shifts or tasks requiring higher-order feature interactions.
